messages.content: I applied for a Senior Sales Manager position at Linkvertise. I have met the CEO several times and he is keen to have me on board. Recently I met the members of the sales team. We had a great chat and shared many common ideas. They were very excited to have me on board. Today I met the CEO again.  It seems that his sales team have told him that they have doubts about me. Reason 1 about the distance from my place of work, which is an hour's drive, and reason 2 about the fact that the position requires some account management tasks and I might not be keen on that. The CEO is now in a position where he doesn't want to go against his team, but at the same time he is keen to take me on. He said he would discuss it again with the team and let me know. What are your thoughts about that? What should I offer and say to the CEO?

It's a delicate situation when there's a disconnect between a CEO's perspective on a candidate and the sales team's concerns. Here are some strategies you might consider to navigate this scenario:

1. **Address Concerns Directly:** Reach out to the CEO and request if it's possible to have a meeting with the sales team. In this meeting, address their concerns head-on. Assure them that the commute wouldn't affect your performance or punctuality, and you're fully aware of the account management responsibilities and are eager to take them on.

2. **Propose a Probation Period:** You could offer to work on a probationary period. This would give both you and the sales team a chance to evaluate the working relationship without a long-term commitment. If you can prove your value and alleviate their concerns during this period, it might convince the team that the initial reservations were unfounded.

3. **Demonstrate Flexibility and Commitment:** Show that you're committed to the role by suggesting flexibility in your working arrangement. For instance, you could propose working some days from home, if applicable, or fit your hours around peak traffic times to mitigate the commute issue.

4. **Highlight Your Experience in Account Management:** Clearly communicate your experience and success in account management tasks. Provide examples or case studies where you have excelled in this area to reassure the team of your capabilities and enthusiasm for these tasks.

5. **Provide Assurances About Integration and Teamwork:** Make sure to express how much you value being part of a team and your commitment to integrating smoothly into their culture and workflow.

6. **Seek Feedback:** Ask for specific feedback about the team's concerns and what you might do to assure them of your suitability. It may be that they have misinterpreted your enthusiasm for sales as a lack of interest in management tasks, which you could easily clarify.

7. **Mutual Benefit Argument:** Explain to the CEO and the team how you will bring value to the company and how your experience and skill set will contribute to the growth and success of the sales team. Make it clear that your involvement would be mutually beneficial.

8. **Set Clear Objectives:** Suggest setting clear performance objectives that can be reviewed. This will give the team a measurable standard against which they can judge your contribution to the business.

9. **Continued Open Dialogue:** Stress the importance of ongoing communication with the team and suggest regular check-ins to ensure that any concerns can be addressed early.

10. **Build Internal Support:** If there is anyone on the team who might be an advocate for you, reach out and see if they would be willing to support your cause. Having an internal advocate can sometimes help sway opinions.

Lastly, keep the dialogue with the CEO constructive and positive. Reiterate your keen interest in the role, your willingness to address the team's concerns, and your belief that you can overcome any challenges with their support. The CEO himself seems to see the potential in you, so your objective is to ensure his faith is justified and to build the same level of trust with his team.
Based on your 10 points, give me a text idea I could send to the CEO. Avoid buzzwords and clichés and use a honest, friendly and simple tone.

Certainly! Here's a text idea that you could use as inspiration to send to the CEO:

---

Hello [CEO's Name],

I've spent some time reflecting on our recent conversation and the feedback from the sales team. I understand their concerns about the commute and the account management responsibilities, and I'd genuinely like to address them.

Regarding the distance, I'm confident that it won't impact my dedication or my ability to fulfill my role. I'm used to being on the road and can assure you that it has never hindered my performance in the past.

On the account management front, I want to clarify that I'm truly excited about this aspect of the job. I've always embraced the full spectrum of sales roles and understand the importance of nurturing client relationships just as much as driving new business.

I propose a meet-up with the sales team to discuss these points openly. It would give me the chance to hear their concerns firsthand and demonstrate my commitment to being a team player.

Moreover, I'm open to the idea of a trial period to solidify my fit within the team and the company. This way, we have a chance to measure my contributions objectively.

I value the opportunity at Linkvertise greatly and believe we can achieve fantastic things together. I'm eager to bring my experience to the table and work alongside the team to reach our collective goals.

If it's agreeable, I'd love to discuss this further and explore how we can move ahead in a way that everyone feels confident and excited about the future.

Best regards,
[Your Name]

---

This note aims to be respectful, addresses the main concerns, and offers proactive solutions to demonstrate your willingness to integrate with the team and contribute to the company's success.
Add somehow within this text a special message addressed to the Sales Team.

Certainly! Here's the text with a special message included for the sales team.

---

Hello [CEO's Name],

I've been giving our recent conversation considerable thought, especially the concerns raised by the sales team regarding my commute and the account management aspect of the role.

Straight to the point about the commute—I'm prepared and accustomed to it. It's something I've managed before and have always maintained a high performance level despite the distance.

As for the account management responsibilities, I must stress that it's a part of the job I'm very enthusiastic about. I believe that building and nurturing client relationships are as essential as driving sales, and I've had substantial experience and success in this area.

I wonder if it might be beneficial for me to convey this directly to the sales team. I'd welcome the opportunity for an open dialogue with them to listen to their concerns and to share my passion and vision for how we can work together. I believe that teamwork is founded on communication and trust, and I'd love to start building those from the very beginning.

Perhaps we could consider a probationary period to objectively evaluate my integration into the team. This could be a great way for everyone to gauge compatibility and witness firsthand the energy and expertise I'm eager to contribute.

I value the chance to join Linkvertise significantly, and I'm keen to reinforce to the sales team that I'm here to support and enhance our collective successes, not just my own.

If you think it would be helpful, I'm ready to discuss how we can proceed in a manner that gives everyone confidence and excitement about what lies ahead.

Looking forward to your thoughts and hopeful for the next steps.

Warm regards,
[Your Name]

---

This version includes a more explicit reach-out to the sales team and reinforces your desire to work closely with them to alleviate any concerns they might have. It communicates your eagerness to foster team spirit and build rapport with the sales team from the onset.
Make it slightly shorter and easy to read.

Of course! Here it is in a more succinct form:

---

Hi [CEO's Name],

I've given our chat and the sales team's concerns a lot of thought. I want to reassure you: the hour drive won't impact my work, and I'm actually excited about managing accounts—I enjoy building lasting client relationships.

Maybe a brief sit-down with the team would help? It would give me a chance to address their concerns directly and show how committed I am to the role.

What do you think about a trial period? It could be a no-risk way for all of us to make sure we’re a great fit.

I'm really enthusiastic about the chance to work with Linkvertise and I'm ready to roll up my sleeves and get to work with everyone.

Let me know if you want to chat more about this.

Thanks,
[Your Name]

---

This shortened version maintains the core message of addressing the concerns and suggests a personal meeting with the team along with a trial period to show your commitment and fit for the role.

- I've then created the text below to the CEO. Review it and bring any improvements:

Hi again, Marc!

I’ve been giving our recent chat considerable thought, especially the concerns raised by the sales team.

Straight to the point about the commute - I’m prepared and accustomed to it. It’s something I’ve managed before and have always maintained a high performance level despite the distance.

As for the account management taks, I must stress that it’s a part of the job I’m very enthusiastic about. I believe that building and nurturing client relationships are as essential as driving sales, and I’ve had substantial experience and success in this area.

Moreover, the trial period will be a great chance for us to see how things mesh, for me to show you what I can bring to the table, and to objectively evaluate my integration into the team. I'm confident we can make this work brilliantly.

I had great time chatting with the Team! I really had the impression that our ideas and vision really clicked. If it’s then agreeable, I’d love to discuss these points openly further with you and the Team, and explore how we can move ahead in a way that everyone feels confident and excited about the future.

Once again, I'm truly pumped about the possibility of joining the Team and making a difference!

Looking forward to your thoughts and hopeful for the next steps.

All the best,

Sebastien

Hi Sebastien,

Your draft to Marc is quite solid—it's candid, addresses the concerns directly, and demonstrates enthusiasm for the role. Here are a few tweaks for clarity and impact:

---

Hi Marc,

I've been reflecting on our conversation and the sales team’s reservations.

Regarding the commute - rest assured, I have a history of managing similar distances without it affecting my performance in the least.

On the topic of account management tasks, I'm not just ready; I'm genuinely excited. Fostering client relationships is something I take as seriously as pursuing sales targets, and I have a track record to prove it.

The trial period sounds like the perfect opportunity for us to confirm how well we work together, and for me to demonstrate the value I know I can add to the team.

My recent discussion with the team left a great impression on me—I felt a strong alignment in our ideas and vision. I’d welcome the chance to communicate further with you and the team, highlighting how I believe we can all move forward together positively and confidently.

I am truly excited about the potential of contributing to the team’s success and eager to take on this challenge.

Looking forward to hearing your thoughts and to discussing the next steps.

Best regards,

Sebastien

---

A few adjustments are made for flow and to maintain professionalism, while still keeping it personable. Good luck with your follow-up!

Certainly, Sebastien. Here's another variation of your message:

---

Hello Marc,

After our recent discussion and understanding the sales team's concerns, I've given everything a lot of thought.

Concerning the commute, I want to alleviate any worries. I have always navigated similar journeys and wouldn't have pursued this opportunity if I wasn’t completely ready to handle it. My track record supports my ability to consistently deliver top performance, regardless of the distance.

As for managing accounts, that's a part of the role I relish. The experience I have in fostering client relationships is extensive, and I view it as integral to achieving sales objectives.

The proposed trial period is an ideal way for us to gauge how we sync up, and for me to showcase my contributions and how seamlessly I can integrate with the team.

The rapport I felt with the team was unmistakable, and it seemed our visions for the future were in harmony. I'm eager to further open this dialogue with you and the team to ensure we’re all moving forward on the same page, with confidence and anticipation for what we can accomplish together.

I’m enthusiastic about the impact I can make and I'm ready to dive into this new challenge with the team.

I’m looking forward to your thoughts and discussing what comes next.

Warm regards,

Sebastien

---

This variation keeps the essence of your original message but is even more focused on addressing the concerns and confirming your readiness and excitement for the role.

I'm also keen on account management – it's as essential to me as driving sales, and I'm experienced in both.

Give me another variation of this text. Use a freindly and approachable tone:

Hi again, Marc!

I've been thinking about our last chat and the reservations of the sales team.

Regarding the commute, rest assured I'm well prepared for it! I wouldn't have pursued this opportunity if I wasn't fully prepared. I have a history of managing similar distances without it affecting my performance in the least.

As for the account management tasks, I'm not only ready, I'm genuinely excited. Building and maintaining customer relationships is just as important as driving sales, and I've had considerable experience and success in this area.

I think the probation period is the perfect opportunity for us to see how we fit together, for me to demonstrate what I can bring to the table and to objectively evaluate my integration into the team. 

I had a great time talking to the team! I really got the impression that our ideas and visions were really in sync. If it's appropriate, I’d welcome the chance to discuss these points openly further with you and the team, and explore how we can move ahead in a way that everyone feels confident and excited about the future.

Once again, I'm really pumped about the possibility of joining the team and making a difference!

Looking forward to your thoughts and hopeful for the next steps.

All the best,

---

Hi Marc,

Just been mulling over our conversation and the team's feedback.

I won't lie, the commute is a bit of a hike, but it's nothing new for me. I've juggled similar trips before and always managed to keep my work spot-on, so I've got this.

And account management? I'm all in. Seriously, I thrive on making those client connections just as much as I do closing deals. Years in the game means I've got a pretty packed toolkit in that department.

The trial period? Sounds like the golden ticket to me. It's a chance to sync up, show off my skills, and see how I meld with the team.

Chatting with the gang was a blast – clicked like a puzzle. If you're game, I'd love to keep the convo rolling. Let's carve out a path that gets us all jazzed for what's ahead.

Can't shake the excitement for the chance to be part of the crew and shake things up!

Eagerly awaiting your next move.

Cheers,
Sebastien

---

Here is the subject line for this text:

Let's Address the Team's Concerns Together!

Give me other ideas

Give me now another variations of this text:

Hi Marc,

I've taken some time to ponder our conversation and the concerns from the sales team.

Here's how I see it:

The commute is a non-issue for me; I'm more than prepared to make it work. It's always been part of my professional life, and it's never held back my performance or commitment.

Regarding account management, I'm all in. It's a part of the job that pumps me up just as much as pushing for those sales numbers. Plus, I've got a solid track record to back up my abilities there.

I see the trial period as a win-win. It'll be a real-time chance for me to demonstrate my skills and how I mesh with the team's dynamic.

The dynamic during our team meeting really struck a chord with me – I felt a genuine connection with everyone's goals and outlook. Open to a deeper dive into our discussion, I’d relish the opportunity for us all to build a shared vision that's energizing and ground-breaking.

I'm buzzing with anticipation at the thought of rolling up my sleeves and getting started with the team.

Eagerly awaiting your feedback and the next chapter.

Warm regards,
Sebastien

---

Give me an A/B test of this text:

Hi again, Marc!

I have been reflecting on our chat and the team's feedback.

Just to clear up any hesitations:

As for the commute, rest assured I'm well prepared for it! I wouldn't have pursued this opportunity if I wasn't fully prepared. 

Regarding the account management tasks, I’m all in! Building and maintaining customer relationships is just as crucial as driving sales ... and I've got extensive experience and success with that.

I then see the trial period as a perfect opportunity for us to see how we fit together, for me to show what I can bring to the table and to objectively see how I fit into the team.

I had a great time talking to the team! I felt that our ideas and ambitions were in sync. If appropriate, I'd welcome the chance to discuss these points further with you and the team, openly, and explore how we can move ahead so that everyone feels confident and excited about the future.

Again, I'm pumped about the opportunity to join the team! Looking forward to your thoughts and hope for the next steps.

All the best,

To conduct an A/B test, you would create two versions (A and B) of the same text, each with slight variations in wording, tone, or structure. Then, you can measure the response to each version to determine which is more effective. Here's how you might set up your A/B test with two different versions based on your text:

**Version A: Focused on Confidence and Experience**

Subject: Eager to Address Team's Feedback and Forge Ahead

Hi Marc,

After our insightful conversation and digesting the sales team's feedback, I'm all set to tackle any concerns.

About the commute – I've been down this road before, literally. I'm equipped for the journey and fully confident that it won't impact my work ethic or results.

When it comes to account management, it's genuinely an area where I excel. Building strong customer bonds has been pivotal in my sales approach, and I've got a proven track record in this arena.

Let's use the trial period as a proving ground. It’s the right platform to demonstrate my abilities and how seamlessly I’ll blend into the Linkvertise culture.

I enjoyed connecting with the team, and I sense we're aligned in our ambitions and approaches. I’d appreciate the opportunity to discuss our visions further and set a course that will leave everyone engaged and optimistic.

Eager to contribute to the team’s success and hit the ground running!

Looking forward to plotting the next steps together.

Warmest,
Sebastien

**Version B: Emphasizing Team Integration and Excitement**

Subject: Driving Forward: Commute and Account Management Excitement

Hi again, Marc!

Reflecting on our recent talk and the team's input, I'm ready to address any hesitations head-on.

I want to reassure you about the commute; it's a challenge I'm well acquainted with and have always managed without compromising performance.

As for account management, it's an aspect of sales I approach with gusto – spearheading client relationships is just as rewarding for me as closing deals, and my experience here speaks volumes.

The proposed trial feels like the ideal route for all of us to assess our synergy and for me to highlight my integration into the Linkvertise team.

Meeting the team was inspiring – our shared aspirations and creative energy were palpable. I'm keen to delve into these discussions further with you and the team, charting a path that ignites confidence and collective excitement.

I am buzzing about the prospect of being part of such a dynamic team and I'm ready for whatever comes next.

Awaiting your thoughts and raring to go.

Best,
Sebastien

---

Each version maintains the essence of your message but uses different angles to achieve the same end. One focuses on confidence and experience, while the other emphasizes integration and excitement. Testing both (sent to similar but separate audience segments) would let you see which approach elicits the most positive response from your recipients.
